      - Because alphas_ sign was fixed at fit time and transform uses that same alphas_, output signs are consistent deterministically. No break.

  - Method: inverse_transform (when fit_inverse_transform True)
    - fit creates self.dual_coef_ by solving K + alpha*I using X_transformed (which was built from the already-flipped alphas_), so dual_coef_ matches the sign convention. inverse_transform uses self.dual_coef_ directly; no mismatch.

- Conclusion for reverse operations:
  No complementary operations will be inconsistent after flipping self.alphas_. Because all uses of alphas_ are derived from the stored self.alphas_, flipping them at the moment of assignment makes transform and inverse_transform consistent.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods in KernelPCA and how they behave after the fix:

  - __init__: unchanged.
  - _pairwise/_get_kernel: unchanged.
  - _fit_transform:
    - After eigen-decomposition, we call svd_flip on self.alphas_. This is safe: alphas_ has shape (n_samples, n_components). We must guard against zero components (n_components==0) — svd_flip expects non-zero inner dims; so we will only call if self.alphas_.size > 0 (or self.lambdas_.size>0).
    - The rest of the method's returns are unchanged.
  - _fit_inverse_transform:
    - Uses X_transformed created from self.alphas_ (already flipped) — works fine.
  - fit:
    - After _fit_transform is called, fit will compute X_transformed = dot(self.alphas_, sqrt_lambdas) if fit_inverse_transform True. Because alphas_ are already sign-fixed, the computed X_transformed will use the sign convention and _fit_inverse_transform will produce dual_coef_ consistent with that sign. No break.
    - self.X_fit_ is stored as before.
  - fit_transform:
    - Calls fit() then computes X_transformed = self.alphas_ * sqrt(self.lambdas_). With alphas_ flipped inside fit, fit_transform returns deterministic output. Works fine.
  - transform:
    - Uses self.alphas_ / sqrt(lambdas_) — consistent with flipped alphas_. Works fine.
  - inverse_transform:
    - Uses self.dual_coef_; works fine as fit computed dual_coef_ from sign-consistent X_transformed.

- Edge cases:
  - If after remove_zero_eig or n_components is None there are zero components (i.e., no eigenvectors left), our guard must avoid calling svd_flip on empty arrays. Implementation detail: check that self.alphas_.shape[1] > 0 before calling svd_flip.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new public methods are required.
- KernelPCA only needs to call the existing svd_flip helper (or equivalent sign-correction logic) after eigenvectors are computed. No addition of inverse helpers or additional overrides is needed because all usages read the stored, sign-fixed alphas_.

Concrete changes to add (complete fix):

1) Add import in sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py near other imports at top:
   from ..utils.extmath import svd_flip

   (If the coding style of the file groups imports by relative depth, follow the same; the file currently imports check_random_state, check_array from ..utils.* earlier.)

2) Insert sign-fixing code at the end of KernelPCA._fit_transform, after sorting and after removing zero eigenvectors. The code placement should be right after the block:

   # sort eigenvectors in descending order
   indices = self.lambdas_.argsort()[::-1]
   self.lambdas_ = self.lambdas_[indices]
   self.alphas_ = self.alphas_[:, indices]

   # remove eigenvectors with a zero eigenvalue
   if self.remove_zero_eig or self.n_components is None:
       self.alphas_ = self.alphas_[:, self.lambdas_ > 0]
       self.lambdas_ = self.lambdas_[self.lambdas_ > 0]

Add:

   # enforce deterministic sign of eigenvectors (make largest-abs-entry positive)
   if self.alphas_.size:
       # use u-based decision: columns of alphas_ are the eigenvectors
       # pass a dummy v matrix that will be ignored
       self.alphas_, _ = svd_flip(self.alphas_,
                                 np.ones((self.alphas_.shape[1], 1)),
                                 u_based_decision=True)

- Note: ensure to guard against empty self.alphas_ (e.g., no components) since svd_flip expects non-zero inner dimension.

Patch-style example (diff context):

At top of file (with other imports):
   from ..utils import check_random_state
   from ..utils.validation import check_is_fitted, check_array
   from ..exceptions import NotFittedError
   from ..base import BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin, _UnstableOn32BitMixin
   from ..preprocessing import KernelCenterer
- Add:
   from ..utils.extmath import svd_flip
   from ..metrics.pairwise import pairwise_kernels

Inside _fit_transform, after removal of zero eigenvalues:

   # remove eigenvectors with a zero eigenvalue
   if self.remove_zero_eig or self.n_components is None:
       self.alphas_ = self.alphas_[:, self.lambdas_ > 0]
       self.lambdas_ = self.lambdas_[self.lambdas_ > 0]

   # enforce deterministic sign of eigenvectors (make largest absolute
   # coefficient positive) to avoid sign indeterminacy across runs
   if self.alphas_.size:
       self.alphas_, _ = svd_flip(self.alphas_,
                                 np.ones((self.alphas_.shape[1], 1)),
                                 u_based_decision=True)

Why use svd_flip with a dummy v of shape (n_components, 1)?
- svd_flip is the standardized helper used elsewhere in the codebase. It expects two matrices u and v with matched inner dimensions; we only need the sign correction based on u (self.alphas_), so a small dummy v suffices and we ignore the second output. Using svd_flip reuses the tested logic and keeps consistent behavior with PCA and other decomposition modules.

Edge-case handling and tests:
- Ensure that when self.alphas_.shape[1] == 0 we do not call svd_flip (guard with .size).
- Add tests verifying that repeated calls to KernelPCA.fit_transform on the same data produce outputs with identical signs across runs and across different eigen_solver choices (dense/arpack) and that sign convention matches PCA's svd_flip style (largest absolute element positive).
- Also test that transform() and inverse_transform() are consistent with the stored alphas_; e.g., fit with fit_inverse_transform=True followed by inverse_transform on a transformed vector should produce consistent results regardless of solver or run.

Checkpoint 5: Final report

Root cause
- KernelPCA returns eigenvectors (self.alphas_) directly from linalg.eigh or eigsh. Eigenvectors are sign-indeterminate—v and -v are both valid. KernelPCA did not enforce a deterministic sign convention, so different solver runs, different platform behavior, or parallelization/random initialization could produce eigenvectors that differ by sign across runs. The difference in sign is expected mathematically but undesirable for deterministic reproducibility. PCA and other decomposition classes in scikit-learn use svd_flip to fix sign deterministically; KernelPCA did not.

Every location that needs to change (complete list)
- Primary change:
  - File: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py
    - Add: import svd_flip: from ..utils.extmath import svd_flip
    - Modify KernelPCA._fit_transform: after sorting eigenvectors and (optionally) removing zero-eigenvalue eigenvectors, apply svd_flip (or equivalent sign-correction) to self.alphas_.

- No other files need to be changed. KernelCenterer and other component classes are used but unaffected. The only change is to make eigenvector signs deterministic inside KernelPCA.

Detailed rationale for the change:
- Current behavior: After computing eigenvectors, KernelPCA sets self.alphas_ to the eigenvectors; final output X_transformed is computed directly from those eigenvectors. Because eigenvector signs are arbitrary, output signs vary.
- What the fix does: Force each eigenvector's sign so that the component entry with largest absolute value is positive (same convention as svd_flip uses in PCA). This yields deterministic outputs for the same input across runs.

All assumptions changed and hardcoded values
- Assumption removed: "Eigenvector sign is irrelevant and does not need a deterministic convention." We now enforce a deterministic convention.
- Hardcoded values examined:
  - K.shape[0] > 200 and n_components < 10 solver heuristic: unaffected.
  - eigvals tuple in linalg.eigh: unchanged and unaffected by sign correction.
  - ARPACK initial v0: still used as before; svd_flip handles sign determinism after eigenvectors are computed.

Reverse operation check (writing vs reading)
- fit computes X_transformed from self.alphas_ (flipped) and fit_inverse_transform uses that X_transformed to compute dual_coef_ — both use the same sign-convention. transform and inverse_transform will use the sign-fixed alphas_ and dual_coef_, so they remain consistent.

Verification that every existing method still works after the fix
- _fit_transform: only change is sign correction; everything else unchanged.
- fit: uses corrected alphas_ when computing X_transformed for fit_inverse_transform.
- fit_transform: returns X_transformed constructed from corrected alphas_.
- transform: uses corrected alphas_ and lambdas_; output deterministic.
- inverse_transform: uses dual_coef_ solved from corrected X_transformed; consistent.

New methods
- No new public methods needed. Use existing svd_flip helper. If desired, could inline equivalent code (argmax abs by column and multiply by signs), but svd_flip is already in codebase and is the standard helper.

Suggested patch (concise)
- In sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py:
  - Add: from ..utils.extmath import svd_flip to imports.
  - After the block that sorts and removes zero eigenvalues in _fit_transform, add:

    # enforce deterministic sign of eigenvectors (make largest abs entry positive)
    if self.alphas_.size:
        self.alphas_, _ = svd_flip(self.alphas_,
                                  np.ones((self.alphas_.shape[1], 1)),
                                  u_based_decision=True)

Notes & follow-ups
- Tests should be added to the test suite to ensure KernelPCA returns deterministically-signed components across repeated runs and across eigen_solver choices.
- There is still an unavoidable permutation ambiguity if eigenvalues are equal or nearly equal (e.g., multiple components with identical eigenvalues). svd_flip only fixes sign, not ordering; ordering is determined by sorting eigenvalues. For exact multiplicities, eigenvectors are only defined up to orthonormal basis on the subspace; addressing that would require more sophisticated canonicalization (beyond scope).
- If desired, one could implement the sign-correction without importing svd_flip, e.g.:

    if self.alphas_.size:
        max_abs_cols = np.argmax(np.abs(self.alphas_), axis=0)
        signs = np.sign(self.alphas_[max_abs_cols, range(self.alphas_.shape[1])])
        signs[signs == 0] = 1
        self.alphas_ *= signs

  This is equivalent to the u_based_decision branch of svd_flip, and avoids creating a dummy v.

Summary
- Root cause: eigenvector sign indeterminacy not corrected.
- Fix: apply a deterministic sign convention (use svd_flip or equivalent) to self.alphas_ at the end of KernelPCA._fit_transform.
- Change required in: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py (imports and _fit_transform).
- No other files need modification for correctness. Add tests to lock down the behavior.

If you’d like, I can produce a precise patch/diff for kernel_pca.py (with exact line numbers and formatting) that you can apply or open a PR with.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: KernelPCA returns components whose signs flip between runs because eigenvectors (self.alphas_) are not canonicalized; the code assumes eigenvector sign is irrelevant and leaves the sign indeterminate.
- Root cause & location: eigenvectors are produced and stored in sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py → KernelPCA._fit_transform (attributes self.alphas_ / self.lambdas_). The method never applies a deterministic sign convention (unlike PCA, which uses svd_flip).
- Fix strategy: after eigen-decomposition and sorting (and after removing zero eigenvalues) enforce a deterministic sign convention on self.alphas_ (make the largest-absolute-value entry in each component positive), e.g. by calling sklearn.utils.extmath.svd_flip or equivalent column-wise sign correction.
- Coverage: changing KernelPCA._fit_transform (plus adding import of svd_flip) covers all places that read/write alphas_ (fit, fit_transform, transform, inverse_transform, _fit_inverse_transform) because they all derive from the stored self.alphas_. No other files need modification.
- Hardcoded constants: no numeric constants need changing. The solver-selection heuristics (K.shape[0] > 200 and n_components < 10) and ARPACK v0 initialization remain valid; the fix operates after the solver returns eigenvectors.
- Gaps in the investigation:
  - Did not execute a concrete numeric trace (single example arrays) to prove transform/inverse_transform numeric equality post-fix.
  - Did not verify behavior for degenerate/equal eigenvalues where subspace basis (and permutation) can be non-deterministic — sign-fixing alone does not resolve subspace-basis ambiguity.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Sign indeterminacy left uncorrected in KernelPCA._fit_transform
What: KernelPCA computes eigenvectors and stores them in self.alphas_ without applying a deterministic sign convention; eigenvectors can be ±v, so outputs (fit_transform/transform) can flip sign across runs.
Where: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : KernelPCA._fit_transform
Source: reading kernel_pca.py via get_file_context (agent step: opened sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py)

### Finding 2: PCA and other decomposition modules canonicalize signs using svd_flip
What: PCA uses sklearn.utils.extmath.svd_flip to make component signs deterministic (largest-abs-entry positive); KernelPCA does not use this helper.
Where: sklearn/decomposition/pca.py uses from ..utils.extmath import svd_flip; call U, V = svd_flip(U, V)
Why it matters: Consistency with existing conventions in the codebase and reuse of tested helper logic.
Source: reading pca.py and extmath.svd_flip (agent step: opened pca.py and extmath.py)

### Finding 3: All users of alphas_ downstream will inherit sign choice
What: fit, fit_transform, transform, and _fit_inverse_transform all read self.alphas_ (and self.lambdas_) to compute outputs; flipping alphas_ in place will automatically keep all dependent methods consistent.
Where: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : fit, fit_transform, transform, _fit_inverse_transform
Why it matters: A single canonicalization point (after eigen-decomposition) is sufficient; no other methods must be changed.
Source: reading kernel_pca.py internals (agent step: inspected methods in the file)

### Finding 4: Edge cases and gaps not fully checked
What: The investigation did not run a concrete numeric trace verifying transform/inverse_transform numerically after applying the flip; it also did not examine equal-eigenvalue cases where eigenvectors may rotate/permutate (sign-fixing doesn't fix subspace-basis ambiguity).
Why it matters: Tests are needed to ensure no unexpected numeric regressions and to document remaining non-determinism when eigenvalues are tied.
Source: gap discovered during fix-challenge (agent step: checkpoint analysis)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py:KernelPCA._fit_transform
- Wrong assumption: The code assumes eigenvector sign can be left arbitrary and does not need a deterministic convention.
- Evidence:
  - _fit_transform computes eigen-decomposition (scipy.linalg.eigh or eigsh) and sets self.alphas_ directly after sorting (see assignment self.alphas_ = ... and self.lambdas_ = ...). No svd_flip or equivalent appears in this file.
  - Observed bug report: identical results except sign flips between runs.
  - PCA uses svd_flip elsewhere to enforce determinism; KernelPCA lacks that step.
  - Exact variables: self.alphas_ (shape (n_samples, n_components)), self.lambdas_ (shape (n_components,))
- Concrete change (exact code to add):
  - Add import at top of file (with other imports):
    from ..utils.extmath import svd_flip
  - After sorting and removing zero eigenvalues, add (guarding empty case):
    # enforce deterministic sign of eigenvectors (make largest-abs-entry positive)
    if self.alphas_.size:
        # use u-based decision; pass small dummy v and ignore it
        self.alphas_, _ = svd_flip(
            self.alphas_,
            np.ones((self.alphas_.shape[1], 1)),
            u_based_decision=True
        )
- Related locations:
  - sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : fit (uses self.alphas_ to compute X_transformed for fit_inverse_transform)
  - sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : fit_transform (returns self.alphas_ * np.sqrt(self.lambdas_))
  - sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : transform (returns np.dot(K, self.alphas_ / np.sqrt(self.lambdas_)))
  - sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : _fit_inverse_transform (receives X_transformed built from alphas_)

### Location: sklearn/utils/extmath.py:svd_flip
- Wrong assumption (in KernelPCA context): KernelPCA assumed no need to canonicalize signs, but extmath.svd_flip provides the canonicalization standard used by PCA.
- Evidence:
  - svd_flip implements "make largest absolute entry positive" rule: it computes argmax(abs(u), axis=0) and multiplies columns by sign to ensure positivity.
  - PCA uses svd_flip; thus canonicalization is already implemented and tested in the codebase.
- Related locations:
  - sklearn/decomposition/pca.py : uses svd_flip(U, V)
  - Other modules: truncated_svd.py, incremental_pca.py, etc., which call svd_flip for deterministic behavior.

### Location: sklearn/preprocessing/data.py:KernelCenterer.transform
- Wrong assumption: None in terms of sign; but sanity check: KernelCenterer alters K in-place and KernelPCA depends on centered kernel K when computing eigenvectors.
- Evidence:
  - KernelCenterer.transform centers kernels by subtracting precomputed means and works with shapes [n_samples1, n_samples2], using self.K_fit_rows_ and self.K_fit_all_.
  - This class does not alter eigenvector sign and is orthogonal to the sign issue — but it's a critical component in the pipeline whose outputs feed _fit_transform.
- Related locations:
  - sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py : _fit_transform (calls KernelCenterer.transform)

### Investigation gaps / tests to add (evidence locker)
- Gap: no numeric trace with concrete arrays (small X example) was executed to show transform/inverse_transform produce numerically identical results before vs after fixing sign behavior. Add unit tests using a fixed small X and both eigen_solver='dense' and eigen_solver='arpack' (random_state fixed) to assert deterministic sign output.
- Gap: equal eigenvalues (multiplicity) not canonicalized — sign-fixing does not solve eigen-subspace rotation/permutation. Document in tests that exact reproducibility is not guaranteed for degenerate eigenvalues.
- Gap: ensure the code guards empty self.alphas_ (n_components may be 0 after remove_zero_eig), i.e., do not call svd_flip on empty inner dimensions.

482: def svd_flip(u, v, u_based_decision=True):
483:     """Sign correction to ensure deterministic output from SVD.
484: 
485:     Adjusts the columns of u and the rows of v such that the loadings in the
486:     columns in u that are largest in absolute value are always positive.
487: 
488:     Parameters
489:     ----------
490:     u : ndarray
491:         u and v are the output of `linalg.svd` or
492:         `sklearn.utils.extmath.randomized_svd`, with matching inner dimensions
493:         so one can compute `np.dot(u * s, v)`.
494: 
495:     v : ndarray
496:         u and v are the output of `linalg.svd` or
497:         `sklearn.utils.extmath.randomized_svd`, with matching inner dimensions
498:         so one can compute `np.dot(u * s, v)`.
499: 
500:     u_based_decision : boolean, (default=True)
501:         If True, use the columns of u as the basis for sign flipping.
502:         Otherwise, use the rows of v. The choice of which variable to base the
503:         decision on is generally algorithm dependent.
504: 
505: 
506:     Returns
507:     -------
508:     u_adjusted, v_adjusted : arrays with the same dimensions as the input.
509: 
510:     """
511:     if u_based_decision:
512:         # columns of u, rows of v
513:         max_abs_cols = np.argmax(np.abs(u), axis=0)
514:         signs = np.sign(u[max_abs_cols, range(u.shape[1])])
515:         u *= signs
516:         v *= signs[:, np.newaxis]
517:     else:
518:         # rows of v, columns of u
519:         max_abs_rows = np.argmax(np.abs(v), axis=1)
520:         signs = np.sign(v[range(v.shape[0]), max_abs_rows])

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I will verify each diagnostic claim in the draft report against the source code and the investigation findings. I will (a) inspect the KernelPCA implementation to confirm where eigenvectors/eigenvalues are computed and how they are used, (b) confirm whether any sign-canonicalization is applied there, (c) confirm how downstream public methods use the stored eigenvectors/eigenvalues, and (d) confirm the repository convention (svd_flip used elsewhere, e.g. PCA).

I will reference specific lines/locations from the code to justify each verification step.

1) Verify KernelPCA eigen-decomposition and storage of self.alphas_ / self.lambdas_

- What I checked: KernelPCA._fit_transform in sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py.
- Evidence from code:
  - eigen-decomposition calls:
    - dense branch: "self.lambdas_, self.alphas_ = linalg.eigh(K, eigvals=(...))" (kernel_pca.py lines 200-203).
    - arpack branch: "self.lambdas_, self.alphas_ = eigsh(K, n_components, ...)" (lines 206-211).
  - Sorting of eigenpairs:
    - "indices = self.lambdas_.argsort()[::-1]" then
    - "self.lambdas_ = self.lambdas_[indices]" and "self.alphas_ = self.alphas_[:, indices]" (lines 213-216).
  - Optional removal of zero eigenvalues:
    - "if self.remove_zero_eig or self.n_components is None: self.alphas_ = self.alphas_[:, self.lambdas_ > 0]; self.lambdas_ = self.lambdas_[self.lambdas_ > 0]" (lines 218-221).
- Conclusion: The code computes and stores eigenvalues in self.lambdas_ and eigenvectors in self.alphas_ exactly as described in the draft. The lines above confirm where the decomposition and assignments occur.

2) Verify absence of deterministic sign canonicalization in KernelPCA

- What I checked: kernel_pca.py for any call to svd_flip or equivalent sign canonicalization after eigen-decomposition.
- Evidence from code:
  - After computing and sorting eigenvectors, there is no call to svd_flip or any sign-flipping helper; the method simply removes zero eigenvalues and returns (lines 213-223). No sign-canonicalization is present in this file.
- Conclusion: The claim that KernelPCA does not apply a deterministic sign convention to eigenvectors is supported by the code.

3) Verify how downstream outputs depend on self.alphas_ and self.lambdas_

- What I checked: methods fit, fit_transform, transform and _fit_inverse_transform in kernel_pca.py.
- Evidence from code:
  - fit (when fit_inverse_transform=True) constructs X_transformed as:
    - "sqrt_lambdas = np.diag(np.sqrt(self.lambdas_))" and "X_transformed = np.dot(self.alphas_, sqrt_lambdas)" (lines 256-258).
  - fit_transform returns:
    - "X_transformed = self.alphas_ * np.sqrt(self.lambdas_)" (line 278) and returns X_transformed (lines 276-283).
  - transform returns:
    - "K = self._centerer.transform(self._get_kernel(X, self.X_fit_))" then
    - "return np.dot(K, self.alphas_ / np.sqrt(self.lambdas_))" (lines 298-299).
  - _fit_inverse_transform uses X_transformed to build a kernel and solve for dual_coef_ (lines 231-234); it stores X_transformed_fit_ (line 234).
- Conclusion: The draft's formulas (fit_transform uses alphas_ * sqrt(lambdas_ and transform uses dot(K, alphas_ / sqrt(lambdas_))) are accurate representations of the code. Because these methods use the stored self.alphas_ directly, any sign choice in alphas_ is propagated to user-visible outputs.

4) Verify the repository convention: svd_flip usage in PCA and other decompositions

- What I checked: sklearn/decomposition/pca.py and sklearn/utils/extmath.py.
- Evidence from code:
  - PCA calls svd_flip after linalg.svd: "U, S, V = linalg.svd(X, full_matrices=False)" then "U, V = svd_flip(U, V)" (pca.py lines 435-438).
  - The svd_flip implementation shows it flips signs so that the largest absolute entry in each column of u is positive (extmath.py lines 482-517 show the function and description; lines 511-516 show the u_based_decision branch using argmax(abs(u), axis=0) and applying signs).
  - svd_flip is used in other decomposition modules (search shows uses in truncated_svd, incremental_pca, PLS, etc.).
- Conclusion: The codebase does have a convention / helper for making SVD outputs deterministic; KernelPCA does not use it, making KernelPCA inconsistent with these other modules.

5) Confirm the draft's secondary/investigation findings are represented and whether additional affected locations exist

- The investigation noted that all methods that consume self.alphas_ will inherit the arbitrary sign choice (fit, fit_transform, transform, _fit_inverse_transform). I verified all these usages above and they are present in kernel_pca.py. The draft RootCause lists these locations; that is complete with respect to discovered places in this file.
- The investigation also noted gaps:
  - No numeric trace of transform/inverse_transform equivalence post-canonicalization (a testing gap). This is an investigation/test gap, not additional code evidence; it should be preserved as a caveat.
  - Degenerate/equal eigenvalues: sign-canonicalization does not resolve the non-deterministic choice of basis within a degenerate eigenspace (subspace rotations/permutations). I checked the eigen-decomposition calls (linalg.eigh / eigsh). These solvers can produce non-deterministic bases for repeated eigenvalues, and sign flips only address per-vector ± ambiguity, not general subspace ambiguity. This is a valid caveat and should be included.
- No other files in the repo appear to compute KernelPCA.alphas_ differently; search for "alphas_" did not show other consumers outside kernel_pca.py that relate to KernelPCA.

6) Check for any claims in the draft that are unsupported or require softening

- The draft's statement "The code assumes eigenvector sign is irrelevant and therefore allows ±v ambiguity to persist" is an interpretation; the code never sets a sign convention, so the interpretation is reasonable and supported.
- The draft included explicit code-change suggestions and an exact snippet for a concrete change (LOW_LEVEL "Concrete change (exact code to add)"). The final report must not include fix prescriptions or code snippets. These must be removed or rewritten as diagnosis-only (i.e., absence of canonicalization) in RootCause.
- The draft's RootCause item 2 mentions formulas for fit_transform and transform — these are correct and belong as evidence of propagation of sign choice; they are acceptable in RootCause when phrased descriptively (not prescriptive).

7) Field-discipline check (restructuring where necessary)

- Description should concisely state the observable bug (sign flips across runs). The draft Description is fine.
- RootCause must list incorrect assumptions and code locations only, without prescribing fixes. The current draft RootCause mixes diagnosis and "fix strategy" and "concrete change". I will rewrite RootCause to:
  - State the root facts (no sign canonicalization in KernelPCA._fit_transform).
  - State the specific locations that hold the incorrect assumption or are affected: KernelPCA._fit_transform, fit, fit_transform, transform, _fit_inverse_transform in sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py.
  - State the relationship/formulas showing how self.alphas_ feeds outputs (to justify why sign choice is user-visible).
  - State the repository convention (svd_flip used elsewhere) and that KernelPCA is inconsistent.
  - Add the investigation caveats (degenerate eigenvalues, test gaps) as part of RootCause or a separate note inside RootCause — guidelines require RootCause include every finding sharing the same root cause pattern; these caveats are related and should be included.
- StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, ObservedBehavior in the draft are focused appropriately and need no substantive change.

8) Completeness check for other affected code paths

- KernelCenterer is used in _fit_transform to center K before decomposition (line 183-185). KernelCenterer does not perform sign flips and is orthogonal to sign issue; the draft mentions this and the investigation noted it as sanity check. It is not an additional affected location for sign canonicalization, but the pipeline dependency is relevant; RootCause should not claim Centerer causes the sign flips.
- I searched for any other code that writes or expects kernel_pca.alphas_ with a sign convention; none found. The affected outputs are the public methods that directly return or compute with alphas_ (fit_transform, transform, fit when fit_inverse_transform=True, inverse_transform uses dual_coef_ computed from X_transformed — chained but consistent).

Summary of verification conclusions

- The main diagnostic claims in the draft are correct:
  - KernelPCA computes eigenvectors as self.alphas_ and does not apply any deterministic sign canonicalization.
  - Fit/fit_transform/transform use self.alphas_ and self.lambdas_ in the formulas shown; therefore, sign ambiguity in alphas_ is directly visible in outputs.
  - The repo does have a standard helper svd_flip used in PCA/other decompositions for deterministic sign flipping; KernelPCA does not follow it.
- The draft included prescriptive code-change suggestions and exact code snippets; those are not supported as part of a diagnosis and must be removed from RootCause and other fields.
- Investigation caveats (no numeric trace, handling of equal eigenvalues/subspace ambiguity) are valid and should be included as part of the RootCause findings (as limitations/remaining non-determinism that sign canonicalization alone may not eliminate).
- All locations dependent on the same root cause (self.alphas_ being left with undetermined sign) are represented: KernelPCA._fit_transform, KernelPCA.fit, KernelPCA.fit_transform, KernelPCA.transform, KernelPCA._fit_inverse_transform. These must be listed with equal weight.

Phase 2 — Revised JSON report

I have made the following edits to the draft report:
- Removed any concrete code-suggestion or patch text (no fixes or implementation guidance).
- Rewrote RootCause to be a focused diagnosis: state the incorrect assumption(s), list every affected location equally, include the key formulas showing how alphas_ propagates into user-visible outputs, and include the investigation caveats about equal eigenvalues and test gaps.
- Kept Description, StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, ObservedBehavior focused on observable behaviour.
- Added explicit code evidence references (file and line hints) to the "evidence" list below.

bug_report.Title: KernelPCA returns components with nondeterministic sign (per-run sign flips)
bug_report.Description: When running KernelPCA (for example with the rbf kernel) multiple times on the same data, the returned projected components are identical in magnitude but one or more component columns may be inverted in sign between runs (per-component ±1 sign flips).
bug_report.RootCause:
1) KernelPCA does not enforce a deterministic sign convention after eigen-decomposition: In sklearn/decomposition/kernel_pca.py, KernelPCA._fit_transform computes eigenvalues and eigenvectors and assigns them to self.lambdas_ and self.alphas_ (dense branch: linalg.eigh; arpack branch: eigsh), sorts them, and optionally removes zero eigenvalues, but contains no canonicalization step that fixes per-eigenvector sign. (See kernel_pca.py lines where self.lambdas_ and self.alphas_ are assigned and sorted.)

2) Public methods expose self.alphas_ directly so the arbitrary sign choice is user-visible: the stored eigenvectors are used directly when producing outputs: fit_transform returns X_transformed = self.alphas_ * np.sqrt(self.lambdas_) (kernel_pca.py: fit_transform), fit (when fit_inverse_transform=True) computes X_transformed = np.dot(self.alphas_, diag(sqrt(self.lambdas_))) (kernel_pca.py: fit), and transform returns np.dot(K, self.alphas_ / np.sqrt(self.lambdas_)) (kernel_pca.py: transform). Because these formulas use self.alphas_ as computed by the eigen-solver, any per-vector ± ambiguity in self.alphas_ appears as sign flips in the returned projections.

3) KernelPCA is inconsistent with the decomposition modules that apply a deterministic sign rule: elsewhere in the codebase, SVD-based decompositions use sklearn.utils.extmath.svd_flip (which enforces the largest-absolute-entry positive rule) to remove the per-vector sign ambiguity (e.g. PCA calls svd_flip after linalg.svd). KernelPCA lacks an equivalent canonicalization step, which explains the observed per-run sign inconsistencies and makes KernelPCA behavior differ from other decompositions.

4) Remaining non-determinism caveat: sign-canonicalization addresses the ±v ambiguity for individual eigenvectors but does not remove all sources of non-determinism when eigenvalues are (nearly) equal: in the presence of degenerate eigenvalues, the eigensolver can return any orthonormal basis of the eigenspace (rotations/permutations), so exact per-vector reproducibility is not guaranteed by sign-fixing alone. The investigation also did not include a numeric trace demonstrating equivalence of transform/inverse_transform post-canonicalization; that is a test/coverage gap to be validated separately.
StepsToReproduce: Import KernelPCA and load your dataset X.
Instantiate pca = KernelPCA(n_components=7, kernel='rbf', copy_X=False, n_jobs=-1).
Call pca.fit_transform(X) and save the result (run A).
Repeat pca.fit_transform(X) in a new Python process or after re-instantiating pca and save the result (run B).
Compare run A and run B: observe that corresponding component columns are identical in magnitude but some columns are multiplied by -1.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Repeated runs of KernelPCA with the same input data and equivalent environment produce the same projected components (no unexpected per-component sign flips).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Between runs on the same dataset, KernelPCA returns projections that match in magnitude but some component columns are inverted in sign (e.g. one run has [-0.44457617 -0.18155886 ... 0.18124364], another run has [0.44457617 0.18155886 ... -0.18124364]).

"""Utilities for meta-estimators"""
# Author: Joel Nothman
#         Andreas Mueller
# License: BSD

from abc import ABCMeta, abstractmethod
from operator import attrgetter
from functools import update_wrapper
import numpy as np

from ..utils import safe_indexing
from ..base import BaseEstimator

__all__ = ['if_delegate_has_method']


class _BaseComposition(BaseEstimator, metaclass=ABCMeta):
    """Handles parameter management for classifiers composed of named estimators.
    """
    @abstractmethod
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def _get_params(self, attr, deep=True):
        out = super().get_params(deep=deep)
        if not deep:
            return out
        estimators = getattr(self, attr)
        out.update(estimators)
        for name, estimator in estimators:
            if hasattr(estimator, 'get_params'):
                for key, value in estimator.get_params(deep=True).items():
                    out['%s__%s' % (name, key)] = value
        return out

    def _set_params(self, attr, **params):
        # Ensure strict ordering of parameter setting:
        # 1. All steps
        if attr in params:
            setattr(self, attr, params.pop(attr))
        # 2. Step replacement
        items = getattr(self, attr)
        names = []
        if items:
            names, _ = zip(*items)
        for name in list(params.keys()):
            if '__' not in name and name in names:
                self._replace_estimator(attr, name, params.pop(name))
        # 3. Step parameters and other initialisation arguments
        super().set_params(**params)
        return self

    def _replace_estimator(self, attr, name, new_val):
        # assumes `name` is a valid estimator name
        new_estimators = list(getattr(self, attr))
        for i, (estimator_name, _) in enumerate(new_estimators):
            if estimator_name == name:
                new_estimators[i] = (name, new_val)
                break
        setattr(self, attr, new_estimators)

    def _validate_names(self, names):
        if len(set(names)) != len(names):
            raise ValueError('Names provided are not unique: '
                             '{0!r}'.format(list(names)))
        invalid_names = set(names).intersection(self.get_params(deep=False))
        if invalid_names:
            raise ValueError('Estimator names conflict with constructor '
                             'arguments: {0!r}'.format(sorted(invalid_names)))
        invalid_names = [name for name in names if '__' in name]
        if invalid_names:
            raise ValueError('Estimator names must not contain __: got '
                             '{0!r}'.format(invalid_names))


class _IffHasAttrDescriptor:
    """Implements a conditional property using the descriptor protocol.

    Using this class to create a decorator will raise an ``AttributeError``
    if none of the delegates (specified in ``delegate_names``) is an attribute
    of the base object or the first found delegate does not have an attribute
    ``attribute_name``.

    This allows ducktyping of the decorated method based on
    ``delegate.attribute_name``. Here ``delegate`` is the first item in
    ``delegate_names`` for which ``hasattr(object, delegate) is True``.

    See https://docs.python.org/3/howto/descriptor.html for an explanation of
    descriptors.
    """
    def __init__(self, fn, delegate_names, attribute_name):
        self.fn = fn
        self.delegate_names = delegate_names
        self.attribute_name = attribute_name

        # update the docstring of the descriptor
        update_wrapper(self, fn)

    def __get__(self, obj, type=None):
        # raise an AttributeError if the attribute is not present on the object
        if obj is not None:
            # delegate only on instances, not the classes.
            # this is to allow access to the docstrings.
            for delegate_name in self.delegate_names:
                try:
                    delegate = attrgetter(delegate_name)(obj)
                except AttributeError:
                    continue
                else:
                    getattr(delegate, self.attribute_name)
                    break
            else:
                attrgetter(self.delegate_names[-1])(obj)

        # lambda, but not partial, allows help() to work with update_wrapper
        out = lambda *args, **kwargs: self.fn(obj, *args, **kwargs)
        # update the docstring of the returned function
        update_wrapper(out, self.fn)
        return out


def if_delegate_has_method(delegate):
    """Create a decorator for methods that are delegated to a sub-estimator

    This enables ducktyping by hasattr returning True according to the
    sub-estimator.

    Parameters
    ----------
    delegate : string, list of strings or tuple of strings
        Name of the sub-estimator that can be accessed as an attribute of the
        base object. If a list or a tuple of names are provided, the first
        sub-estimator that is an attribute of the base object will be used.

    """
    if isinstance(delegate, list):
        delegate = tuple(delegate)
    if not isinstance(delegate, tuple):
        delegate = (delegate,)

    return lambda fn: _IffHasAttrDescriptor(fn, delegate,
                                            attribute_name=fn.__name__)


def _safe_split(estimator, X, y, indices, train_indices=None):
    """Create subset of dataset and properly handle kernels.

    Slice X, y according to indices for cross-validation, but take care of
    precomputed kernel-matrices or pairwise affinities / distances.

    If ``estimator._pairwise is True``, X needs to be square and
    we slice rows and columns. If ``train_indices`` is not None,
    we slice rows using ``indices`` (assumed the test set) and columns
    using ``train_indices``, indicating the training set.

    Labels y will always be indexed only along the first axis.

    Parameters
    ----------
    estimator : object
        Estimator to determine whether we should slice only rows or rows and
        columns.

    X : array-like, sparse matrix or iterable
        Data to be indexed. If ``estimator._pairwise is True``,
        this needs to be a square array-like or sparse matrix.

    y : array-like, sparse matrix or iterable
        Targets to be indexed.

    indices : array of int
        Rows to select from X and y.
        If ``estimator._pairwise is True`` and ``train_indices is None``
        then ``indices`` will also be used to slice columns.

    train_indices : array of int or None, default=None
        If ``estimator._pairwise is True`` and ``train_indices is not None``,
        then ``train_indices`` will be use to slice the columns of X.

    Returns
    -------
    X_subset : array-like, sparse matrix or list
        Indexed data.

    y_subset : array-like, sparse matrix or list
        Indexed targets.

    """
    if getattr(estimator, "_pairwise", False):
        if not hasattr(X, "shape"):
            raise ValueError("Precomputed kernels or affinity matrices have "
                             "to be passed as arrays or sparse matrices.")
        # X is a precomputed square kernel matrix
        if X.shape[0] != X.shape[1]:
            raise ValueError("X should be a square kernel matrix")
        if train_indices is None:
            X_subset = X[np.ix_(indices, indices)]
        else:
            X_subset = X[np.ix_(indices, train_indices)]
    else:
        X_subset = safe_indexing(X, indices)

    if y is not None:
        y_subset = safe_indexing(y, indices)
    else:
        y_subset = None

    return X_subset, y_subset

class ColumnTransformer(_BaseComposition, TransformerMixin):
    """Applies transformers to columns of an array or pandas DataFrame.

    EXPERIMENTAL: some behaviors may change between releases without
    deprecation.

    This estimator allows different columns or column subsets of the input
    to be transformed separately and the features generated by each transformer
    will be concatenated to form a single feature space.
    This is useful for heterogeneous or columnar data, to combine several
    feature extraction mechanisms or transformations into a single transformer.

    Read more in the :ref:`User Guide <column_transformer>`.

    .. versionadded:: 0.20

    Parameters
    ----------
    transformers : list of tuples
        List of (name, transformer, column(s)) tuples specifying the
        transformer objects to be applied to subsets of the data.

        name : string
            Like in Pipeline and FeatureUnion, this allows the transformer and
            its parameters to be set using ``set_params`` and searched in grid
            search.
        transformer : estimator or {'passthrough', 'drop'}
            Estimator must support `fit` and `transform`. Special-cased
            strings 'drop' and 'passthrough' are accepted as well, to
            indicate to drop the columns or to pass them through untransformed,
            respectively.
        column(s) : string or int, array-like of string or int, slice, \
boolean mask array or callable
            Indexes the data on its second axis. Integers are interpreted as
            positional columns, while strings can reference DataFrame columns
            by name.  A scalar string or int should be used where
            ``transformer`` expects X to be a 1d array-like (vector),
            otherwise a 2d array will be passed to the transformer.
            A callable is passed the input data `X` and can return any of the
            above.

    remainder : {'drop', 'passthrough'} or estimator, default 'drop'
        By default, only the specified columns in `transformers` are
        transformed and combined in the output, and the non-specified
        columns are dropped. (default of ``'drop'``).
        By specifying ``remainder='passthrough'``, all remaining columns that
        were not specified in `transformers` will be automatically passed
        through. This subset of columns is concatenated with the output of
        the transformers.
        By setting ``remainder`` to be an estimator, the remaining
        non-specified columns will use the ``remainder`` estimator. The
        estimator must support :term:`fit` and :term:`transform`.

    sparse_threshold : float, default = 0.3
        If the output of the different transformers contains sparse matrices,
        these will be stacked as a sparse matrix if the overall density is
        lower than this value. Use ``sparse_threshold=0`` to always return
        dense.  When the transformed output consists of all dense data, the
        stacked result will be dense, and this keyword will be ignored.

    n_jobs : int or None, optional (default=None)
        Number of jobs to run in parallel.
        ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend` context.
        ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary <n_jobs>`
        for more details.

    transformer_weights : dict, optional
        Multiplicative weights for features per transformer. The output of the
        transformer is multiplied by these weights. Keys are transformer names,
        values the weights.

    Attributes
    ----------
    transformers_ : list
        The collection of fitted transformers as tuples of
        (name, fitted_transformer, column). `fitted_transformer` can be an
        estimator, 'drop', or 'passthrough'. In case there were no columns
        selected, this will be the unfitted transformer.
        If there are remaining columns, the final element is a tuple of the
        form:
        ('remainder', transformer, remaining_columns) corresponding to the
        ``remainder`` parameter. If there are remaining columns, then
        ``len(transformers_)==len(transformers)+1``, otherwise
        ``len(transformers_)==len(transformers)``.

    named_transformers_ : Bunch object, a dictionary with attribute access
        Read-only attribute to access any transformer by given name.
        Keys are transformer names and values are the fitted transformer
        objects.

    sparse_output_ : boolean
        Boolean flag indicating wether the output of ``transform`` is a
        sparse matrix or a dense numpy array, which depends on the output
        of the individual transformers and the `sparse_threshold` keyword.

    Notes
    -----
    The order of the columns in the transformed feature matrix follows the
    order of how the columns are specified in the `transformers` list.
    Columns of the original feature matrix that are not specified are
    dropped from the resulting transformed feature matrix, unless specified
    in the `passthrough` keyword. Those columns specified with `passthrough`
    are added at the right to the output of the transformers.

    See also
    --------
    sklearn.compose.make_column_transformer : convenience function for
        combining the outputs of multiple transformer objects applied to
        column subsets of the original feature space.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
    >>> from sklearn.preprocessing import Normalizer
    >>> ct = ColumnTransformer(
    ...     [("norm1", Normalizer(norm='l1'), [0, 1]),
    ...      ("norm2", Normalizer(norm='l1'), slice(2, 4))])
    >>> X = np.array([[0., 1., 2., 2.],
    ...               [1., 1., 0., 1.]])
    >>> # Normalizer scales each row of X to unit norm. A separate scaling
    >>> # is applied for the two first and two last elements of each
    >>> # row independently.
    >>> ct.fit_transform(X)    # doctest: +NORMALIZE_WHITESPACE
    array([[0. , 1. , 0.5, 0.5],
           [0.5, 0.5, 0. , 1. ]])

    """
    _required_parameters = ['transformers']

    def __init__(self, transformers, remainder='drop', sparse_threshold=0.3,
                 n_jobs=None, transformer_weights=None):
        self.transformers = transformers
        self.remainder = remainder
        self.sparse_threshold = sparse_threshold
        self.n_jobs = n_jobs
        self.transformer_weights = transformer_weights

    @property
    def _transformers(self):

        Internal list of transformer only containing the name and
        transformers, dropping the columns. This is for the implementation
        of get_params via BaseComposition._get_params which expects lists
        of tuples of len 2.

        return [(name, trans) for name, trans, _ in self.transformers]

    @_transformers.setter
    def _transformers(self, value):

            (name, trans, col) for ((name, trans), (_, _, col))
            in zip(value, self.transformers)]

    def get_params(self, deep=True):
        """Get parameters for this estimator.

        Parameters
        ----------
        deep : boolean, optional
            If True, will return the parameters for this estimator and
            contained subobjects that are estimators.

        Returns
        -------
        params : mapping of string to any
            Parameter names mapped to their values.
        """
        return self._get_params('_transformers', deep=deep)

    def set_params(self, **kwargs):

        Valid parameter keys can be listed with ``get_params()``.

        Returns
        -------

        """
        self._set_params('_transformers', **kwargs)

    def _iter(self, fitted=False, replace_strings=False):
        """
        Generate (name, trans, column, weight) tuples.

        If fitted=True, use the fitted transformers, else use the
        user specified transformers updated with converted column names
        and potentially appended with transformer for remainder.

        if fitted:
            transformers = self.transformers_
        else:
            # interleave the validated column specifiers
            transformers = [
                (name, trans, column) for (name, trans, _), column
                in zip(self.transformers, self._columns)

            # add transformer tuple for remainder
            if self._remainder[2] is not None:
                transformers = chain(transformers, [self._remainder])
        get_weight = (self.transformer_weights or {}).get

        for name, trans, column in transformers:
            if replace_strings:
                # replace 'passthrough' with identity transformer and
                # skip in case of 'drop'
                if trans == 'passthrough':
                    trans = FunctionTransformer(
                        validate=False, accept_sparse=True,
                        check_inverse=False)

                    continue
                elif _is_empty_column_selection(column):
                    continue

            yield (name, trans, column, get_weight(name))

    def _validate_transformers(self):
        if not self.transformers:
            return

        names, transformers, _ = zip(*self.transformers)

def _parallel_fit_estimator(estimator, X, y, sample_weight=None):
    """Private function used to fit an estimator within a job."""
    if sample_weight is not None:
        estimator.fit(X, y, sample_weight=sample_weight)
    else:
        estimator.fit(X, y)
    return estimator


class VotingClassifier(_BaseComposition, ClassifierMixin, TransformerMixin):

        The number of jobs to run in parallel for ``fit``.
        ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend` context.
        ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary <n_jobs>`
        for more details.

    flatten_transform : bool, optional (default=True)
        Affects shape of transform output only when voting='soft'
        If voting='soft' and flatten_transform=True, transform method returns
        matrix with shape (n_samples, n_classifiers * n_classes). If
        flatten_transform=False, it returns
        (n_classifiers, n_samples, n_classes).

    Attributes
    ----------
    estimators_ : list of classifiers
        The collection of fitted sub-estimators as defined in ``estimators``
        that are not `None`.

    named_estimators_ : Bunch object, a dictionary with attribute access
        Attribute to access any fitted sub-estimators by name.

        .. versionadded:: 0.20

    classes_ : array-like, shape = [n_predictions]
        The classes labels.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
    >>> from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB
    >>> from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, VotingClassifier
    >>> clf1 = LogisticRegression(solver='lbfgs', multi_class='multinomial',
    ...                           random_state=1)
    >>> clf2 = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=50, random_state=1)
    >>> clf3 = GaussianNB()
    >>> X = np.array([[-1, -1], [-2, -1], [-3, -2], [1, 1], [2, 1], [3, 2]])
    >>> y = np.array([1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2])
    >>> eclf1 = VotingClassifier(estimators=[
    ...         ('lr', clf1), ('rf', clf2), ('gnb', clf3)], voting='hard')
    >>> eclf1 = eclf1.fit(X, y)
    >>> print(eclf1.predict(X))
    [1 1 1 2 2 2]
    >>> np.array_equal(eclf1.named_estimators_.lr.predict(X),
    ...                eclf1.named_estimators_['lr'].predict(X))
    True
    >>> eclf2 = VotingClassifier(estimators=[
    ...         ('lr', clf1), ('rf', clf2), ('gnb', clf3)],
    ...         voting='soft')
    >>> eclf2 = eclf2.fit(X, y)
    >>> print(eclf2.predict(X))
    [1 1 1 2 2 2]
    >>> eclf3 = VotingClassifier(estimators=[
    ...        ('lr', clf1), ('rf', clf2), ('gnb', clf3)],
    ...        voting='soft', weights=[2,1,1],
    ...        flatten_transform=True)
    >>> eclf3 = eclf3.fit(X, y)
    >>> print(eclf3.predict(X))
    [1 1 1 2 2 2]
    >>> print(eclf3.transform(X).shape)
    (6, 6)
    """
    _required_parameters = ['estimators']

    def __init__(self, estimators, voting='hard', weights=None, n_jobs=None,
                 flatten_transform=True):
        self.estimators = estimators
        self.voting = voting
        self.weights = weights
        self.n_jobs = n_jobs
        self.flatten_transform = flatten_transform

    @property
    def named_estimators(self):
        return Bunch(**dict(self.estimators))

    def fit(self, X, y, sample_weight=None):
        """ Fit the estimators.

        Parameters
        ----------
        X : {array-like, sparse matrix}, shape = [n_samples, n_features]
            Training vectors, where n_samples is the number of samples and
            n_features is the number of features.

        y : array-like, shape = [n_samples]
            Target values.

        sample_weight : array-like, shape = [n_samples] or None
            Sample weights. If None, then samples are equally weighted.
            Note that this is supported only if all underlying estimators
            support sample weights.

        Returns
        -------
        self : object
        """
        if isinstance(y, np.ndarray) and len(y.shape) > 1 and y.shape[1] > 1:
            raise NotImplementedError('Multilabel and multi-output'
                                      ' classification is not supported.')

        if self.voting not in ('soft', 'hard'):

                             % self.voting)

        if self.estimators is None or len(self.estimators) == 0:

                                 ' should be a list of (string, estimator)'

                len(self.weights) != len(self.estimators)):

            for clf in clfs if clf is not None)

        self.named_estimators_ = Bunch(**dict())
        for k, e in zip(self.estimators, self.estimators_):
            self.named_estimators_[k[0]] = e

    def _weights_not_none(self):

        if self.weights is None:

"""
Base class for ensemble-based estimators.
"""

# Authors: Gilles Louppe
# License: BSD 3 clause

import numpy as np
import numbers

from ..base import clone
from ..base import BaseEstimator
from ..base import MetaEstimatorMixin
from ..utils import check_random_state
from ..utils._joblib import effective_n_jobs
from abc import ABCMeta, abstractmethod

MAX_RAND_SEED = np.iinfo(np.int32).max


def _set_random_states(estimator, random_state=None):
    """Sets fixed random_state parameters for an estimator

    Finds all parameters ending ``random_state`` and sets them to integers
    derived from ``random_state``.

    Parameters
    ----------

    estimator : estimator supporting get/set_params
        Estimator with potential randomness managed by random_state
        parameters.

    random_state : int, RandomState instance or None, optional (default=None)
        If int, random_state is the seed used by the random number generator;
        If RandomState instance, random_state is the random number generator;
        If None, the random number generator is the RandomState instance used
        by `np.random`.

    Notes
    -----
    This does not necessarily set *all* ``random_state`` attributes that
    control an estimator's randomness, only those accessible through
    ``estimator.get_params()``.  ``random_state``s not controlled include
    those belonging to:

        * cross-validation splitters
        * ``scipy.stats`` rvs
    """
    random_state = check_random_state(random_state)
    to_set = {}
    for key in sorted(estimator.get_params(deep=True)):
        if key == 'random_state' or key.endswith('__random_state'):
            to_set[key] = random_state.randint(MAX_RAND_SEED)

    if to_set:
        estimator.set_params(**to_set)


class BaseEnsemble(BaseEstimator, MetaEstimatorMixin, metaclass=ABCMeta):
    """Base class for all ensemble classes.

    Warning: This class should not be used directly. Use derived classes
    instead.

    Parameters
    ----------
    base_estimator : object, optional (default=None)
        The base estimator from which the ensemble is built.

    n_estimators : integer
        The number of estimators in the ensemble.

    estimator_params : list of strings
        The list of attributes to use as parameters when instantiating a
        new base estimator. If none are given, default parameters are used.

    Attributes
    ----------
    base_estimator_ : estimator
        The base estimator from which the ensemble is grown.

    estimators_ : list of estimators
        The collection of fitted base estimators.
    """
    # overwrite _required_parameters from MetaEstimatorMixin
    _required_parameters = []

    @abstractmethod
    def __init__(self, base_estimator, n_estimators=10,
                 estimator_params=tuple()):
        # Set parameters
        self.base_estimator = base_estimator
        self.n_estimators = n_estimators
        self.estimator_params = estimator_params

        # Don't instantiate estimators now! Parameters of base_estimator might
        # still change. Eg., when grid-searching with the nested object syntax.
        # self.estimators_ needs to be filled by the derived classes in fit.

    def _validate_estimator(self, default=None):
        """Check the estimator and the n_estimator attribute, set the
        `base_estimator_` attribute."""
        if not isinstance(self.n_estimators, (numbers.Integral, np.integer)):
            raise ValueError("n_estimators must be an integer, "
                             "got {0}.".format(type(self.n_estimators)))

        if self.n_estimators <= 0:
            raise ValueError("n_estimators must be greater than zero, "
                             "got {0}.".format(self.n_estimators))

        if self.base_estimator is not None:
            self.base_estimator_ = self.base_estimator
        else:
            self.base_estimator_ = default

        if self.base_estimator_ is None:
            raise ValueError("base_estimator cannot be None")

    def _make_estimator(self, append=True, random_state=None):
        """Make and configure a copy of the `base_estimator_` attribute.

        Warning: This method should be used to properly instantiate new
        sub-estimators.
        """
        estimator = clone(self.base_estimator_)
        estimator.set_params(**{p: getattr(self, p)
                                for p in self.estimator_params})

        if random_state is not None:
            _set_random_states(estimator, random_state)

        if append:
            self.estimators_.append(estimator)

        return estimator

    def __len__(self):
        """Returns the number of estimators in the ensemble."""
        return len(self.estimators_)

    def __getitem__(self, index):
        """Returns the index'th estimator in the ensemble."""
        return self.estimators_[index]

    def __iter__(self):
        """Returns iterator over estimators in the ensemble."""
        return iter(self.estimators_)


def _partition_estimators(n_estimators, n_jobs):
    """Private function used to partition estimators between jobs."""
    # Compute the number of jobs
    n_jobs = min(effective_n_jobs(n_jobs), n_estimators)

    # Partition estimators between jobs
    n_estimators_per_job = np.full(n_jobs, n_estimators // n_jobs,
                                   dtype=np.int)
    n_estimators_per_job[:n_estimators % n_jobs] += 1
    starts = np.cumsum(n_estimators_per_job)

    return n_jobs, n_estimators_per_job.tolist(), [0] + starts.tolist()

Thought: Checkpoint 1: Identify the bug

- What I extracted from the report
  - Key class: Pipeline (from sklearn.pipeline)
  - Symptom: len(pipe) raises an error (so Pipeline doesn't implement __len__)
  - Reproducer: pipe = Pipeline([('anova', SelectKBest(...)), ('svc', SVC(...))]); len(pipe)

- What I searched for and why
  - I searched for class Pipeline to inspect its implementation (to see whether __len__ exists and how steps are stored).
  - I searched for _BaseComposition (Pipeline's parent) to see whether the sequence protocol could be implemented there so all composition classes get consistent behavior.

- Files found
  - sklearn/pipeline.py (contains Pipeline and FeatureUnion)
  - sklearn/utils/metaestimators.py (contains _BaseComposition)
  - Also sibling classes inheriting _BaseComposition:
    - sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py (ColumnTransformer)
    - sklearn/ensemble/voting_classifier.py (VotingClassifier)
    - FeatureUnion in pipeline.py

- Source inspected
  - I read the full implementation of sklearn/pipeline.py
  - I read full sklearn/utils/metaestimators.py
  - I read sibling implementations in sklearn/compose/_column_transformer.py and sklearn/ensemble/voting_classifier.py to check consistency.

- Clear statement of the bug
  - The immediate cause: Pipeline does not implement __len__; therefore calling len(pipe) raises TypeError: object of type 'Pipeline' has no len().
  - The primary class responsible: Pipeline (sklearn/pipeline.py). But multiple composition-like classes inherit from _BaseComposition and would be better served by a common implementation so the fix is complete and consistent.

Checkpoint 2: Read the full file and catalog everything

I read the entire file sklearn/pipeline.py.

Classes defined in this file (and their key info):

Notes: Pipeline stores its steps in self.steps as a list of (name, estimator) tuples; __getitem__ and slicing behavior are implemented, but __len__ is missing (which is why len(pipe) fails).

2) FeatureUnion
- Parent classes: _BaseComposition and TransformerMixin
- Class-level attributes:
  - _required_parameters = ["transformer_list"]
- Instance attributes set in __init__:

- Main methods:
  - get_params, set_params
  - _validate_transformers, _iter
